old_claim.note: Theater Ticketing Systems is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: The printed quarto reproduces a stage play and its title page identifies the acting companies that performed it.

source_shape.source_locator: Object record and title-page transcription: identifies the 1594 London quarto as a printed play and names three acting companies that had performed it.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The surviving first quarto is both a printed publication and a text explicitly associated with stage performances by named companies.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That title-page evidence establishes the theatrical work as the content lineage of the printed artifact without importing paid admission into publication.

ontology_before: Printed Theater Scripts directly depended on Theater Ticketing Systems, which blurred publication of play texts with paid-entry theater commerce.
ontology_after: Printed English Play Quartos depend on theater as their dramatic-performance lineage and on printing as their constitutive production method, while ticketing and copyright privileges remain separate contexts.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: printed_theater_scripts no longer directly depends on theater_ticketing_systems and now directly depends on theater.
would_reject_if: The node is rescoped to commercial theater admission or box-office operations rather than printed scripts.
A source shows paid ticketing systems were necessary for the early publication and circulation of play texts.
The node is broadened from printed play quartos to handwritten performance scripts without revisiting the printing dependency.
short_reason: Printed scripts require theater and print publication, not ticketing infrastructure.
